← the Atlas

Electricity — identified, and their progress

analysis · evergreen ·JUN 05, 2026

A tracker for kolu's electricities — infrastructure pulled out from beneath the app into its own thing — and where each stands.

Contents

  • The electricities
  • What the table is really a list of — one contract, four boundaries
  • The table misses an entire kind: invariant electricities
  • A third family: appliance electricities (blob → safe view)
  • The seam the families share: addressing (a reference that outlives what it points at)
  • The real test: does the method generate, or only recognise?
    • The procedure — run it on any app, before any code
  • The metaphor, made generative: axes in, not decoration
  • Newly identified this pass — the engine, turned on
    • ⚡ createOwnedResource — reactive-owner correctness (Family B) · extract-now
    • 🔌 terminal.attach — surface’s last hand-rolled snapshot · narrow
    • 🗄️ migrate-on-load — schema evolution at rest (Family B, server) · candidate
  • Next: extract @kolu/solid-xterm
  • Considered — not electricity

What counts as electricity

Löwy’s receptacle — hide the volatility behind a socket you plug into. Three tests at once: ① domain-agnostic — no terminal/canvas/git/xterm leaks into the interface; ② hides a hard volatility — transport, reconnect, GPU-context loss, persistence — not a 3-line ===; ③ graduates — ships as @kolu/* and a different app plugs in. The proof of ③ is real, not aspirational. @kolu/surface is kolu’s own client↔server transport first — and it has since picked up a second consumer in srid/drishti (a fleet process-monitor; browser ↔ Bun ↔ ssh-agent) that vendors it from this repo and declares its own per-host schema with none of kolu’s domain. The second consumer isn’t the point of the extraction — it’s the proof it was real electricity: surface never noticed the domain changed.

How a session mis-identifies electricity — three traps, learned the hard way

The live example: asked to add back/forward to the Code-tab preview, a session proposed extracting a history stack. Wrong three ways, each caught only by re-asking the test above — and the solid-browser plan is the corrected end-to-end.

  • Naming an organ, not the concept. A history stack is a transformer — a working part inside a larger thing. The electricity was the browser (location + history + link-nav + render-host); history is one chamber of it. Test: is the name self-describing standalone? “history” → history of what?; “browser” → obvious. If you must append “…of X” to explain the name, you’ve named a part, not the receptacle.
  • A module in client/src/ is not electricity. Location is structure (Hickey): a generic-looking helper living inside the app is part of the app, however agnostic the code reads. Electricity is its own package, the dependency arrow pointing out — client → @kolu/x, never back. (createSharedRoot sits in client/src marked “publish?” — a leaf awaiting graduation, not a precedent for calling in-app modules electricity.)
  • Agnostic ≠ electricity — apply test ② honestly. A clean, reusable, agnostic primitive that hides only a bounded algorithm (a stack, a debounce, an ===) is a leaf (nonempty/html-escape-tier), not a receptacle. “I found a tidy generic module” is the feeling that precedes the mistake. Ask what hard volatility it hides — transport, GPU-context loss, sandboxing, reconnect — before reaching for @kolu/.

“Decompose based on volatility.” Functional decomposition splits a system by what it does; volatility-based decomposition isolates each area likely to change behind a stable interface — the way a body keeps blood-pressure, pulse, and salinity volatility “encapsulated behind the service called the heart.” An electricity, here, is one such volatility pulled out into its own package. — Juval Löwy, Volatility-Based Decomposition (excerpt from Righting Software).

This note does two jobs at once, and they pull in opposite directions. As an inventory it tracks what kolu has pulled out from under the app (the table). As a method it must generate those extractions, not merely record them — because an inventory that only recognises electricity in hindsight is a museum, not a tool. So the real test is forward (“does it generate, or only recognise?”): could it have produced surface from “a client and a server talk over a wire” before surface existed?

The lens that does both jobs is three families of volatility — every electricity below is exactly one of them, and the un-named ones hide in the seams between:

  • A · Boundary — a live value across a place you don’t control (network · process · host · origin · and time).
  • B · Invariant — a rule run identically at every call-site, silent when forgotten.
  • C · Appliance — a typed blob turned into a safe, addressable view.

Read the table as the inventory; read the sections after it as the method.

The electricities

ElectricityOwns (volatility)PRProgress
@kolu/surfaceLive client↔server state — Cell/Collection/Stream/Event over oRPC, reconnect, fine-grained reconcile. #805 done · 2nd consumer: drishti
@kolu/surface-appThe app shell for surface apps — fresh delivery + server/build identity + the connection/update lifecycle model + desktop-install. #1154 done · 2nd consumer: drishti
@kolu/surface-nix-hostHostSession — nix copy a closure to a host, realise, run --stdio over ssh. #984 done · also in drishti
@kolu/artifact-sdkTwo volatilities under one roof — the agnostic anchoring core (W3C quote re-find; speaks only Range/Document/ShadowRoot) and the sandboxed-iframe parent↔frame bridge (opaque-origin postMessage, now carrying comments + in-frame navigation + back/forward input). See the re-eval note below. #922 done re-eval
@kolu/transcript-core + transcript-htmlTranscript model + rendering. #744 done
@kolu/solid-pierreSolidJS adapters for Pierre tree/diff. #823 done
@kolu/solid-markdownUntrusted-markdown → safe HTML — the marked → DOMPurify → Shiki pipeline + href policy. The sanitiser is the volatility (XSS surface moves with every CVE).—done · 2nd consumer: solid-fileview
@kolu/pty-hostPTY process lifecycle — node-pty child, @xterm/headless mirror, VT-derived taps (cwd/title/command/foreground). Agnostic to what runs in the shell; its contract schema still lives in kolu-common (③-caveat below).—done ③-caveat
persistedPrefValidated localStorage (validate-on-read; closed the zoom-NaN + maximize bugs). #1089 done
@kolu/log · @kolu/html-escape · @kolu/url-shape · nonempty · memorable-names · terminal-themesZero/near-zero-dep leaves every package imports without dragging the domain tree. url-shape (hasOwnScheme) is the textbook leaf — carved out so solid-markdown’s href policy and solid-browser’s resolver share it with no arrow between them. terminal-themes (okLab nearest-match over the iTerm2 catalog) is a data+algorithm leaf, not a receptacle. #1089 done
@kolu/solid-fileviewSource↔Rendered toggle + renderer dispatch. Honest verdict: a composition, not a receptacle — it arranges solid-markdown/solid-pierre, hides no hard volatility. Shipped as @kolu/* for reuse, but leaf-tier on test ②.—leaf (mislabeled by package scope)
dom/ + injectable isMacwalkShadowRoots relocated to a neutral wall; keybind core no longer reads the UA singleton. #1089 done
createSharedRootLazy app-scoped reactive singleton (7 consumers). 100% agnostic SolidJS. #974 done publish?
@kolu/solid-xterm (createSolidXterm)xterm.js lifecycle — 8-addon orchestration, WebGL single-owner + context-loss recovery, FitAddon resize, owner-correct async dispose across await. #1116 (plan)to build
@kolu/solid-browserThe Code tab is a browser — location/address model, back-forward history, cross-content-type link interception, GitHub-relative resolution, sandboxed-iframe lifecycle. git is an injected resolver; the renderer packages plug in. Plan. #1191 phase 1 primitives shipped · createBrowser+history (P2), ③ proof-pending

What the table is really a list of — one contract, four boundaries

Step back from the package names and most of the “transport” rows collapse into one volatility wearing a different boundary each time:

A reactive value whose source sits on the far side of a boundary you don’t control — where the universal contract for surviving the boundary is snapshot, then deltas, then liveness (reconnect · re-identify · resume).

Boundary you can’t reach acrossWhat changes (the volatility)Where kolu puts the socket
Network (client ↔ server)the link drops, reorders, reconnects@kolu/surface — Cell/Collection/Stream over a retrying link
Process (server ↔ shell)the PTY dies, restarts, flips build-id@kolu/pty-host — served over surface; adds process-lifecycle on top
Host (local ↔ remote machine)the box is unreachable until a closure is realised@kolu/surface-nix-host — realises a daemon, then speaks surface over stdio
Origin (parent ↔ opaque-origin iframe)the frame is sandboxed; event.source is the only trustartifact-sdk’s bridge — hand-rolls snapshot/deltas/liveness again

Read down the last column: three of the four are surface, or surface stretched further. The fourth — the sandboxed-frame bridge — is the only boundary surface’s links can’t cross (opaque origin = no shared transport, postMessage only), so it re-implements the same snapshot/deltas/liveness contract by hand. That hand-rolling isn’t a coincidence; it’s the tell. The real electricity was never “surface the package” — it is the Channel contract itself (snapshot → deltas → liveness), made independent of transport. Surface owns it for websocket/stdio/in-process links; the bridge is simply where it leaked, because no postMessageLink exists yet.

This is the cut the file/module view hides: the same socket is wired four times because four boundaries are volatile, not because four modules exist. It also sharpens the artifact-sdk re-eval (below): the live question isn’t “anchoring vs bridge” — it’s “should the bridge be a surface link rather than a private transport?” — i.e. does the origin boundary deserve a socket of the same shape as the network boundary?

The table misses an entire kind: invariant electricities

Every row above is a boundary electricity — a socket over a place where bytes cross. But the codebase hides a second family the package-shaped view is blind to, because its members aren’t packages — they’re rules enforced by remembering:

An invariant electricity is a correctness rule run as the same cassette of plumbing at N call-sites, where forgetting it produces a silent bug, not a loud one. The volatility isn’t a transport — it’s that the set of call-sites keeps growing, and every new one is a latent failure. (This is .agency/lowy.md’s Layer-0 prompt: the pattern across N call-sites is itself the artifact.)

Invariant run everywhere by handThe silent bug when you forgetEnforce-by-construction receptacle
snapshot → then deltas on every streaming handlerclient renders deltas onto an empty base; a reconnect shows nothingAlready enforce-by-construction — Cell/Collection/Stream handlers (and pollOnEvent) yield the snapshot inside the framework, where domain code can’t skip it (packages/surface/src/server.ts:146-149, packages/surface/src/server.ts:314-335). The lone hand-roll left is the raw-oRPC terminal.attach escape hatch (crop row below).
owner captured before await in any async setuponCleanup lands on a null owner → silent no-op → the subsystem leaks (~900 KB/component; #598/#600/#591)a withOwner-shaped resource: cleanup registers at attach, not via a manual detach() handle
validate on read from any persisted storea stale / NaN / "false" value loads as truthy and corrupts UI statepersistedPref (client) · the state.ts migration ladder (server) — the same invariant in two unconnected homes
silence the expected abort, log the restunmount/navigation spews fake errors that bury the real onesisExpectedCleanupError — one predicate every stream consumer must run

These never surface in a package audit because their correct form deletes code rather than adding a folder. That is the signature of family B: the receptacle is a smaller surface, not a new noun. Where family A asks “what boundary is volatile?”, family B asks “what rule am I trusting myself to remember?” — and both clear Löwy’s bar (a volatility encapsulated so the blast radius of a change stays contained). Note the connection: snapshot→deltas appears in both families — surface (A) defines the contract and (correcting an earlier draft of this note) already enforces it by construction for every Cell/Collection/Stream. The family-B residue is now a single escape hatch — the raw-oRPC terminal.attach — which is exactly the kind of half-built remainder the crop below is for. The two families meet where an electricity is almost finished.

A third family: appliance electricities (blob → safe view)

Families A and B miss the largest cluster of rows. solid-markdown, solid-pierre, transcript-html, the planned solid-xterm, the anchoring core — none is a boundary, none is an invariant. They are appliances: each takes a typed blob and returns a safe, addressable, disposable view, hiding the volatility of the rendering stack itself.

An appliance electricity absorbs two churning things behind a stable (blob, host) → view interface: (1) the rendering tech — which parser, which sanitiser after the next CVE, GPU vs canvas, the next xterm major — and (2) the growing set of content-types. The domain (“render this file in the Code tab”) is the appliance that plugs in; the receptacle never learns it.

ApplianceBlob inVolatility absorbed
@kolu/solid-markdownmarkdown textmarked → DOMPurify → Shiki; the sanitiser moves with every XSS CVE
@kolu/solid-pierrefile / unified diffPierre tree/diff internals, theme churn
@kolu/solid-xterm (to build)PTY byte streamxterm majors, 8-addon orchestration, WebGL context-loss
@kolu/transcript-core+-htmlagent transcripttranscript schema + render
anchoring core (in artifact-sdk)a Range in a rendered docW3C quote re-find as the DOM mutates

Now the whole table has a shape — every electricity is exactly one of three kinds:

  • A · Boundary — a live value across a place you don’t control. (surface · pty-host · nix-host · the bridge transport)
  • B · Invariant — a rule run the same way at every call-site, silent when forgotten. (snapshot→deltas · owner-correctness · validate-on-read · expected-abort)
  • C · Appliance — a typed blob turned into a safe, addressable view. (solid-markdown · solid-pierre · solid-xterm · transcript · anchoring)

And the two compositions on the table — solid-browser and solid-fileview — are revealed for what they are: not electricities but wiring that routes a blob (A: link interception) to the right appliance (C) and owns addressing into the result (location/history). That is exactly why solid-fileview tested as a leaf in the table above: a composition of electricities is not itself one. It also tells you where to look for the next one — the seams between families, where addressing (anchoring, location) spans A and C, are where the un-named infrastructure hides.

The seam the families share: addressing (a reference that outlives what it points at)

Walk into that A×C seam and a fourth thing is hiding — not a package, a capability kolu builds twice and composes by hand:

Addressing — name a point in content and have the name survive the content changing under it. A full address has two levels: which view (location) × where inside it (anchor). A comment is just (location, anchor, payload).

kolu already owns both halves, in different packages:

  • which view → solid-browser’s location/history (survives navigation: the view is replaced)
  • where inside → artifact-sdk’s anchoring core (survives re-render: the DOM mutates; re-find by W3C quote + context)

Nothing owns the pair. “Jump to this comment after a reload,” “deep-link a diff line,” “re-anchor a selection across a re-render and a navigation” each re-compose the two halves at the call-site — a family-B smell (a rule kept by remembering) riding on top of two family-A/C receptacles.

And here is the step that earns the word infrastructure. An anchor is not a string — it is Hickey’s identity over time. The W3C quote is a stable identity; the concrete Range it resolves to is a value that changes every epoch as the document mutates. Re-finding that Range on each render is exactly surface re-delivering a Cell value after a delta — the same snapshot-then-deltas contract, except the boundary it crosses is time, not the network. Which means family A was never really about spatial boundaries: it is “a live identity whose value is re-resolved across a boundary you don’t control” — and network, process, origin, and time are all just values of “which boundary.” Addressing is family A with the boundary dial turned to time.

Over-merge check (the note's own trap ①)

Is “addressing” one receptacle, or a topic-bundle of location + anchor smuggled together? Honest answer: undecided — and that’s the finding. The two halves have genuinely different volatilities (navigation vs DOM-mutation) and may well stay two sockets. What is real and demonstrable is that the pair is used together and owned by no one. Logged as the strongest un-named candidate at the A×C seam — flagged, not declared electricity. (The discipline that catches a forced merge is the same one in the three-traps callout at the top.)

The real test: does the method generate, or only recognise?

A tracker recognises electricity after it ships; a method predicts it. Surface sits in the “done” column — but surface only proves the method if the method would have produced it from “a client and a server talk over a wire,” before anyone wrote a line. So the honest test of this whole note is forward, not backward: run the procedure on kolu as it stands today and write down the electricities it predicts that don’t exist yet. If they later ship and a second consumer plugs in, the method generated them. If the predicted axis never actually moves, the method hallucinated one (see falsification, below). Three standing bets, in falsifiable form:

The method predicts…Derived fromFalsified if…
postMessageLink — the iframe bridge becomes a surface link, so the origin boundary gets the same snapshot/deltas/liveness socket as the network boundaryfamily A: four boundaries, one contract; the bridge is the only hand-rolled onethe opaque-origin boundary genuinely needs a different contract than the network — it doesn’t; both are drop-prone async channels
createOwnedResource — a withOwner-shaped primitive that registers cleanup at attach, so onCleanup can’t land on a null ownerfamily B: owner-before-await, already seen 3× (#598/#600/#591)the SolidJS owner model changes so the await-gap closes upstream, or the call-site set stops growing
@kolu/solid-xterm (already tabled)the xterm lifecycle braida second SolidJS app never embeds xterm (③ never arrives)

The procedure — run it on any app, before any code

Six steps. Inputs are interactions, never features. The falsifiable claim: fed only “a client and a server talk over a wire,” step 6 must emit surface. If it can’t, the method is wrong — and this note is then a catalog of packages wearing a method’s clothes.

  1. Frame the interaction, not the feature. Reject requirements-as-design (Löwy: “any attempt at designing against the requirements will always guarantee pain, because when the requirements change, so will your design”). The only legal input here: a client and a server talk over a wire.
  2. Sweep the two axes of change (both verbatim Löwy): ① same customer over time — what changes for this user as use evolves? (request/response today → server-pushed live state → deltas → reconnect-with-resume). ② all customers at one instant — are they all using the wire the same way? (LAN vs flaky mobile vs proxies that kill long-lived sockets → poll / SSE / WS).
  3. Independence check. Are ① and ② the same change in disguise? No: push semantics over time ≠ transport across customers. Two real axes → a real vault. If they collapse to one, you’ve found functional decomposition wearing a domain hat — stop.
  4. Screen the survivor (three Löwy filters): variable, not volatile? — an if-else/flag can’t span poll→SSE→WS, so it survives. Solution masquerading as requirement? — “add WebSockets” is the Cooking, not the Feeding; climb to the parent: live-state delivery. Open-ended & expensive to contain unless encapsulated? — retrofitting a transport swap across every feature is the loose grenade. Survives.
  5. Name it for the axis, not the tech. WebSocketClient names an activity (it fractures on the first transport swap); LiveStateTransport / surface names the volatility (it doesn’t). The naming test is the cheap proxy for the sweep test in §the metaphor, made generative.
  6. The interface is the receptacle. Verbs that don’t change when the volatility behind them does — snapshot → deltas → liveness; a signal-shaped handle; no AsyncIterable in components. Domain-agnostic, a different app can plug in → you have specified @kolu/surface without having written any kolu.

Step 2 is where functional decomposition dies: the instant you write what changes instead of what it does, surface stops being a design choice and becomes inevitable. (Honest footnote, §hindsight: the method names where the vault is and what its verbs are before code exists — but historically it’s recurring same-shape pain that tells you it’s time to pour it. Foresight names the vault; pain funds it.)

The metaphor, made generative: axes in, not decoration

The reason it’s called electricity isn’t decoration — it’s the procedure read backwards. Löwy lists the power volatilities first — “power can be AC or DC; 110 volts or 220 volts; … 50 hertz or 60 hertz; produced by solar panels …, a generator …, or plain grid connectivity” — and then the receptacle is whatever interface stays bit-identical across every value on every axis. The volatility list is the input; the socket’s geometry is the output (its fixed point). Map the axes straight onto a software seam:

Electricity axis…is software’ssurface’s value of it
Voltage (110/220)wire format / payload shapesnapshot vs delta encoding
Frequency (50/60)transport / push cadencepoll → SSE → WS → WebTransport
Source (solar/grid)provider / originwhich server process authors the state
AC vs DCordering / consistency modelupdate sequencing, resume semantics
Phase (1/3)locality / deploymentLAN vs mobile vs proxied client

The sweep test turns taste into pass/fail: vary each axis end-to-end — grid→solar, poll→WS — and confirm the interface doesn’t change shape at any point. “Needs only minor changes” means it is still leaking; a receptacle doesn’t change at all. The naming test is the cheap proxy: if the best name you can find is a domain noun or an activity gerund (WebSocketClient, BillingManager), you grouped function and the interface will fracture on the first change you forgot to list. (Enumerate-then-sweep is our generative distillation of the analogy — sharper than Löwy’s literal text, which frames discovery as requirements “sweat,” not a mechanical catalog.)

Contract vs carrier — the part that outlasts the metaphor. The electrical contract (sine wave, nominal voltage, pin spacing) is universal; the plug fragmented into ~15 national shapes only because grids were poured before any standard had teeth. In software: spend the enumeration effort on the contract — the atomic verbs and wire invariants (snapshot→deltas) — and treat the carrier (the library, the framework convention) as disposable. surface is a contract; oRPC is a carrier. Confuse the two and you ship a WebSocketClient and rewrite every consumer on the next transport.

Newly identified this pass — the engine, turned on

A method is only worth the name if running it produces electricities you hadn’t tabled. So this section is the output of pointing the procedure at kolu as it stands today — each candidate stated as its axis (what changes), the tell-tale sentence “every time ___ changes, we hand-edit ___,” the kolu evidence (file:line / issue), and an honest verdict. The honesty gate: a generator that only ever says “extract” is a tracker with extra steps, so the crop below defers four and rejects two — and one of them corrects a claim made earlier in this very note.

⚡ createOwnedResource — reactive-owner correctness (Family B) · extract-now

The strongest new find, and a textbook invariant electricity.

  • Axis (what changes): the reactive scope that owns a subscription/effect/resource and guarantees its disposal. SolidJS’s global Owner is null across any await, so an onCleanup registered after an await silently no-ops — and the subsystem leaks with no runtime error, visible only in a heap retainer walk.
  • The sentence: every time a setup grows an await, we hand-edit the getOwner() → runWithOwner() dance — or forget it and leak ~900 KB.
  • Evidence (dated, recurring — not anticipated): the manual dance at packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:463-471; the requirement spelled out at packages/client/src/scrollLock.ts:63-64; and createSharedRoot is the same axis solved inversely (an app-scope owner that outlives any component). Leak history: #591 (190+ orphaned xterm trees), #598, #600, #606.
  • Why now: three independent leaks already paid for it; the receptacle — register cleanup at attach, hold the owner, dispose explicitly — deletes the ceremony at every call-site (the family-B signature). It absorbs createSharedRoot as one configuration of itself.
  • ③ prospect: any SolidJS app that does async setup wants this unchanged — the cleanest second-consumer story of the whole crop.

This promotes the table’s dom/ + createSharedRoot rows from “agnostic helpers awaiting graduation” to two faces of one un-built receptacle.

🔌 terminal.attach — surface’s last hand-rolled snapshot · narrow

Found by correcting this note, not by adding to it.

  • Axis: snapshot-then-deltas (Family B), already owned by surface for every Cell/Collection/Stream.
  • The sentence: every time we add a raw-oRPC stream outside surface, we hand-write if (snapshot) yield snapshot; for await (…) yield delta again.
  • Evidence: the one surviving hand-roll lives at packages/server/src/router.ts:144-151 (terminal.attach) — a raw oRPC procedure, not a surface-managed descriptor, so the framework’s guarantee doesn’t reach it.
  • Verdict — narrow: not a new package; the move is to model terminal.attach as a surface Stream (or route PTY bytes through the existing pollOnEvent shape) so the invariant becomes 100% enforce-by-construction and the escape hatch closes. The electricity here is already built — this is the last meter of wire.

🗄️ migrate-on-load — schema evolution at rest (Family B, server) · candidate

A real receptacle hiding in plain sight inside state.ts — un-named, un-extracted.

  • Axis: the shape of state at rest versus an evolving schema. Persisted JSON written by build N must load cleanly into build N+k.
  • The sentence: every time a persisted field changes shape, we hand-write a migration rung (and bump SCHEMA_VERSION).
  • Evidence: the aggregate schema at packages/server/src/state.ts:88-102, the 23-rung migration ladder (1.1.0 → 1.23.0) at packages/server/src/state.ts:128-442, boot-time validation that refuses to start on mismatch. This is a coherent migrate-on-load receptacle that domain modules read through without ever touching the on-disk layout.
  • ③ prospect: any app persisting versioned state wants exactly this (Conf gives the file; the migration ladder + validate-at-boot + degrade rule is the electricity).

…and the trap it would spring: persistedPref is NOT the same electricity

The seductive move is “unify client persistedPref and server state.ts into one @kolu/validated-store.” Run the independence check — they fail it. Different clocks: the client validates per-entry, on every read, with no migration (a stale value just degrades to its fallback); the server validates once at boot, on an aggregate, through a migration ladder (packages/client/src/persistedPref.ts:56-68 vs packages/server/src/state.ts:128-442). And the wire-validation clock (oRPC checks an incoming patch) is a third, independent one. Same shape (opaque → validate → degrade) ≠ same axis: they change for different reasons. Merging them is the coincidental-duplication trap (legs-on-a-dog = legs-on-a-chair = 4). So: client persistedPref = done (the receptacle, fully adopted — five call-sites, no stragglers); server migrate-on-load = a separate candidate. Two vaults, not one.

Re-evaluating @kolu/artifact-sdk — its electricity framing shifted (open; resolve outside this PR)

“Anchoring” (the original #922 framing) now under-describes the package. It holds two volatilities: (1) the anchoring core (core/ — W3C quote extract/re-find/highlight; pure and agnostic, speaks only Range/Document/ShadowRoot), and (2) the sandboxed-iframe parent↔frame bridge (client/bridge.ts + iframe/ — opaque-origin postMessage with event.source identity as the only trust boundary). The bridge is the harder volatility, and it has quietly accreted clients: comments (selection → SelectMsg → highlights), then in-frame navigation (observeIframeNavigation), then back/forward input (observeIframeHistory, #1191 ). The lens-debate in #1191 already reframed the package’s stated identity to “the in-iframe sandbox bridge, with comments as one client” — but that was a docs fix, not a decomposition.

The open question (work it outside this PR): is “talk to code inside an opaque-origin sandboxed frame” one sound receptacle — a transport-grade volatility with comments / navigation / input as interchangeable clients — or a topic-bundle that should split the agnostic anchoring core from the bridge transport? Note the smell that prompted this: the back/forward client landed in this package only by physical necessity (the one script inside the sandbox is this one), which is exactly the “easy to put it here” the three traps above warn against. Flagged, not decided.

Plus external electricity correctly leaned on instead of hand-rolled: Corvu (dialog/drawer/focus-trap/scroll-lock), @solid-primitives/* (resize-observer, media). Origin: a 41-agent Hickey/Lowy audit (16 candidates) → its “ship now” tier landed in #1089 ; a framework-scale re-test + adversarial skeptic surfaced the missed solid-xterm.

Next: extract @kolu/solid-xterm

The one genuine not-yet-built electricity — mis-filed at first as “terminal domain,” but a SolidJS adapter for xterm.js is agnostic (the .agency/lowy.md §6.5 “what library is this?” test). It owns two declared volatility rows — async-init cleanup (the #598/#600 ~900 KB leaks) and canvas/WebGL lifecycle (the #591 zombie-context hunt, #575/#239/#595).

Inline today in one Terminal.tsx onMount (972 lines): new XTerm packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:472, 8× loadAddon packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:489-509, the WebGL single-owner dance packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:222-285, owner-correct async init packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:463-471, fit/resize packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:179 / packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:750, touch-scroll packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:549-596.

// one primitive (not a topic-bundle, per §6.5):
createSolidXterm({ container, theme, fontSize, canUseWebgl?, enabledAddons?,
  onData?, onResize?, onLinkActivate?, onCustomKeyEvent?, plugins? })
  -> { terminal, searchAddon, hasWebgl, renderer, dispose }

Owns the agnostic core; kolu bits (link provider, soft-keyboard, diagnostics probes, the PTY terminal.attach stream) stay consumer-injected via callbacks. Dissolves ~240–314 lines of Terminal.tsx. Survived the skeptic (“the domain braid runs too deep”) on the interface — those are orchestration concerns, not xterm-lifecycle concerns. Graduates the way surface did: any SolidJS app embedding xterm can plug in.

Considered — not electricity

Looked framework-sized (the audit’s biggest braids), but fail the bar — recorded so they don’t get re-proposed.

CandidateVerdictWhy
defineMutation (optimisticOverlay)helperOne module, one domain (canvas layouts); the write echo is already owned by surface’s metadata subscription. Pending is a UI cache, not a transport axis.
@kolu/fs-watchhelperOwns a refcount/debounce lifecycle, not a streaming transport — no wire contract, Node-bound. A @kolu/shared helper, not a socket.
@kolu/geometrydomain-coupledTileLayout binds it to a server-persisted domain type; a Rect package re-adapts at every boundary — moves the braid, doesn’t dissolve it.
@kolu/surface-publishalready-coveredSurface’s cellHandlers/collectionHandlers/pollOnEvent already own server snapshot+delta. Only terminal.attach is a raw escape hatch.
@kolu/commands (palette)domain-coupledPalette engine is already agnostic; all coupling is isolated in createCommands, which must stay in the app. Extracting deletes ~0 kolu lines.
God-procedure splitrefactorIts biggest dissolvable chunk is solid-xterm (above); the rest is kolu orchestration — a refactor, not a receptacle.

Bar from Juval Löwy, Righting Software (receptacle/volatility) + Rich Hickey, Simple Made Easy (complecting); one-socket-not-a-topic-bundle is .agency/lowy.md §6.5. file:line verified against the codebase; ③ demonstrated by drishti — a second consumer (kolu is the first) that vendors @kolu/surface via npins.

← the Atlas github ↗
